coredata.dc:title: High efficiency gold extraction through photo-luminenscent vesicles self-aggregated by sodium dodecyl sulfate and carbon quantum dots with a visual fluorescent method for Au(III) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Yuqi
Xiang, Zeyang
Lv, Cheng
Wang, Yudong
Xin, Xia
Yang, Yanzhao
coredata.dc:description: Due to the great demand of gold for society innovation, gold recovery technology from second resources are attracting attentions. Herein, we constructed a novel kind of vesicles featuring photo-luminescent for high efficiency gold extraction by combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), reaching 96.8 % of extraction efficiency (E%). A bilayer-membrane structure (>300 nm) with negatively charges (−14.8 ± 1.5 mV) was observed by TEM and characterized by zeta potential measurements. The effect of single factor such as equilibrium time, pH, salinity and vesicles concentration were evaluated. The coordination number 6: 1 was determined by Job’s method. Then, by the combination of 1H NMR, zeta potential analysis and size distribution analysis, anion exchange mechanism was proposed. Because of the fluorescence and strong anti-jamming properties of carbon quantum dots, a novel fluorescent probes for the Au(III) detection in wasted water solution was designed, whose fluorescence cannot be quenched by general base metals, such as Ag+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and so on. Based on the investigation of corresponding gold concentration which could quench the fluorescence of a certain concentration of vesicles, this kind of vesicles could be applied in gold detection. Approximate concentration of gold could be known by watching if the fluorescence quench or not. Or the mixture could be analyzed by PL spectrophotometer, the rough concentration of gold could be known if the fluorescent intensity under 100.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-aggregated system
Vesicles
Gold extraction
Carbon quantum dots
Visual fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recovery of gold by reductive stripping

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Visual images of vesicles prepared by CQDs and SDS/SDC/AOT, “Tr”: “transparent”, “Tur”: “turbid”. The concentrations of surfactants from a1/b1/c1 to a10/b10/c10: 0, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0 mM. (B) Gold(III) extraction efficiency of different combinations.

(A) Effect of extraction equilibrium time on gold(III) extraction efficiency (E%), (B) effect of the concentration of vesicles on gold extraction efficiency (E%), (C) effect of c(H+) on gold extraction efficiency (E%), (D) effect of NaCl/NaBr on gold extraction efficiency (E%).

(A) Zeta potential and (B) diameter distribution of vesicles at different concentrations of SDS, change in (C) zeta potential and (D) size distribution of SDS/CQDs before and after extraction of Au(III), (E) ∼ (G) TEM images of vesicles at 2.0 mM SDS and 0.500 g L−1 CQDs with double-bilayer-membrane structure.

(A) HR-TEM images of CQDs, (B) Job’s method for the analysis of the extraction mechanism of Au(III) by CQDs (Au(III): CQDs molar ratio ranges from 1 to 19), (C) 1H NMR spectra of vesicles@Au(III) and pure vesicles.

(A) Emission spectra at 370 nm of samples of Au(III) and vesicles with continuous vibration. Vibration time: 0, 3, 9, 13, 19, 23, 29, 42, 60 min. (B) variation of the maximum PL intensity excited at 370 nm of vesicles@Au(III) and pure vesicles as a function of vibration time (T). (C) PL intensity of samples and corresponding concentration of Au(III) in water solution. (D) visual images of the corresponding samples in room light and under 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) The first part of each concentration of vesicles ranging from 0.040 g L−1 to 0.200 g L−1 with corresponding “quenching” concentration of Au(III), (b) the second part of each concentration of vesicles ranging from 0.240 to 0.400 g·L−1 with corresponding “quenching” concentration of Au(III), red line: fluorescent intensity equals to 100; red column: pure vesicle solution, black column: vesicles mixed with corresponding concentration of Au(III). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Images of vesicles solution without (On), with (Off) addition of corresponding “quenching” Au(III) solution, and blank (W) under 365 nm UV lamp/roomlight. The concentrations of CQDs combined with SDS from (a) to (j): 0.040, 0.080, 0.120, 0.160, 0.200, 0.240, 0.280, 0.320, 0.360, 0.400 g L−1. The corresponding “quenching” concentration of Au(III) from (a) to (j): 0.024, 0.044, 0.063, 0.097, 0.126, 0.151, 0.175, 0.199, 0.219, 0.233 mM.
Chemical structures of SDS/AOT/SDC and CQDs.
The whole process of vesicle formation and anion-exchange-mechanism gold extraction.

head.ce:title: High efficiency gold extraction through photo-luminenscent vesicles self-aggregated by sodium dodecyl sulfate and carbon quantum dots with a visual fluorescent method for Au(III) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Special Functional Aggregate Materials of Education Ministry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Special Functional Aggregate Materials of Education Ministry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The anion-exchange mechanism of photo-luminenscent vesicles for gold(III) extraction and its fluorescence change.

Due to the great demand of gold for society innovation, gold recovery technology from second resources are attracting attentions. Herein, we constructed a novel kind of vesicles featuring photo-luminescent for high efficiency gold extraction by combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), reaching 96.8 % of extraction efficiency (E%). A bilayer-membrane structure (>300 nm) with negatively charges (−14.8 ± 1.5 mV) was observed by TEM and characterized by zeta potential measurements. The effect of single factor such as equilibrium time, pH, salinity and vesicles concentration were evaluated. The coordination number 6: 1 was determined by Job’s method. Then, by the combination of 1H NMR, zeta potential analysis and size distribution analysis, anion exchange mechanism was proposed. Because of the fluorescence and strong anti-jamming properties of carbon quantum dots, a novel fluorescent probes for the Au(III) detection in wasted water solution was designed, whose fluorescence cannot be quenched by general base metals, such as Ag+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ and so on. Based on the investigation of corresponding gold concentration which could quench the fluorescence of a certain concentration of vesicles, this kind of vesicles could be applied in gold detection. Approximate concentration of gold could be known by watching if the fluorescence quench or not. Or the mixture could be analyzed by PL spectrophotometer, the rough concentration of gold could be known if the fluorescent intensity under 100.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of gold dates back to the very ancient times, even prior to 3400 BC. Even for now, the demand of gold is still increasing, meeting the requirements of the traditional and high-tech manufacturing. While, a significant waste of resources and energy caused by primary low-content ores exploitation is the basic characteristic, as well as with a serious environmental pollution [1,2]. Herein, Au(III) recovery from second resources (for example: wasted computer circuit boards, electronic scrap and waste electroplating solutions) was attracting more attention [3–6].

The conventional separation by liquid-liquid extraction has a great demand of energy, organic solvents, quite a few big problems were left for the post-treatments [7]. With the penetration of the green chemistry, new separation systems like biosorption [8,9], aqueous two-phase system (ATPS) [10,11], micellar-enhanced ultrafiltration (MEUF) are emerging [12]. Combined with the self-aggregated system, MEUF could withheld the ion-loaded micelles to separate the target ions from aqueous solution [13,14]. Self-aggregated vesicles are popular as cargo carriers [15–17], nano materials/nano particles preparation templates [18–20] and novel catalysis [21]. The size distribution and morphology of vesicles are adjusted easily, objects-loaded vesicles could be intercepted by ultrafiltration membranes due to the cake layer filtration effect. Futhermore, its nature: no organic solvents, easy preparation, low toxicity, easy modification, was also attracting us. With introduction of specific molecules structures, vesicles are easy to be modified to achieve multi-stimuli response, e.g., pH [22], oxidation [23], voltage [24].

So, in this paper, a novel kind of vesicles with photo-luminescent (PL) properties was designed by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of nanoparticles with size below 10 nm. With stable photoluminescence (PL) as well as simple preparation [25], biocompatibility [26], it has been widely used in fluorescent dyes preparation [27], photochemical application [28], novel catalysis [29], fluorescence imaging and sensing [30–32]. Nowadays, a series of method to convert the by-products to carbon dots is suggested [33,34]. Combined with CQDs, vesicles featuring PL property were constructed. The gold extraction efficiency by vesicles were evaluated, which were constructed by CQDs and different surfactants. A high efficiency combination by SDS and CQDs were found. A fast extraction process under 5 min were achieved. Its ability and behavior under single factor were investigated. The zeta potential analysis and 1H NMR indicated an anion exchange mechanism. Moreover, by the enlightenment of a method for visual detection using fluorescent off-on mechanism, a strategy of using excellent fluorescence of vesicles to visual detect the amount of Au(III) in water was also developed. Its fluorescent change could be viewed by our naked eyes under UV light. Compared to vesicles constructed by general amphiphilic molecules, vesicles with CQDs introduction is showing the great convenience to the multiple application fields.

The sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, A.R.), sodium cholate (SDC, A.R.), Aerosol OT (AOT, A.R.) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd, (Beijing China). The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized in laboratory, whose procedure has been reported by the paper. The chemical structure of SDS, SDC, AOT and CQDs are shown in Scheme 1 . Gold precursor solution was prepared by dissolving aurum(III) chloride in hydrochloric acid solutions: HAuCl4·4H2O, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd, (Beijing China). Water used in the whole experiment process was triply distilled by a quartz water purification machine, whose conductivity was lower than 1.8 μS cm−1 as measured by a DDSJ-308A type conductivity instrument in laboratory. The temperature used for vesicles preparation and extraction process was 25 °C. The ultrafiltration membranes (25 mm × 0.22 um) were obtained from Shanghai Xinya MEUF experiments. Each sample data was measured for 3–5 times, and the values were averaged.

The morphology of the vesicles was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-100 CXII (Japan)) at 100 KV. Zeta potential and size distribution of the vesicles were measured by a micro electrophoresis meter (JS94H, Zhongchen Ltd. Co., Shanghai). The morphology of the CQDs was observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The C-H shifts used to reveal the mechanism of extraction behavior were analyzed by 1H NMR (Bruker AV400). The flame atomic absorption spectrophotometer (3150, Precision & Scientific Instrument Shanghai Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China) was used to determine the concentration of Au(III) before and after extraction. The operation conditions were as follows: wavelength, 243.1 nm; lamp current, 2 mA; height of the flask, 7.5 mm; slit width, 0.2 nm; flow rate of the burning gas, 2.2 L min−1; flow rate of gas to help burning, 9.4 L min−1. The Intensity of photoluminescence (PL) was measured by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer LS-55).

Take vesicle combinations determination part for example, firstly, the 8 mM × 50 mL SDS/AOT/SDC were prepared along with ultrasonic treatment to make sure all the surfactants are dissolved. 1 g L−1 × 25 mL CQDs solution was dissoluted from a denser solution. Vesicles were prepared by mixing the single surfactant and CQDs in distilled water at room temperature. The total volume of vesicle solution is 5 mL, with single surfactant solution varing and the final CQDs solution keeping at 0.500 g L−1. The self-assembling process relied on the continuous stirring until the obvious cloudy stream disappeared. Then the color of the solutions became turbid with color changing from deep brown to light brown, which means the formation of the vesicles.

Equal volume (2 mL) of Au(III) chloride solution (0.243 mM) and a certain concentration of vesicles were mixed together at room temperature (25 °C), then they were vibrated for 20 min. Vesicles loaded with Au(III) were separated from aqueous system by filter membrane with 0.22 μm pores, because of the cake layer filtration effect. The concentration of Au(III) remaining in solution was determined by flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS). Extraction efficiency (E%) was applied to quantify the ability that vesicles combined with Au(III). The value could be calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
E
( % )
=
( 1 -
M
p
/
M
i
)
× 100
where Mp is the Au(III) amount in the permeated solution and Mi is the Au(III) amount in the initial solution.

Firstly, in order to investigate the effect of different surfactant/CQDs combination on gold extraction efficiency (E%), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, A.R.), sodium cholate (SDC, A.R.), Aerosol OT (AOT, A.R.) were used as ligand components. Various surfactant/CQDs combinations and ratios were processed. The concentration of CQDs was fixed at 0.500 g L−1 while that of surfactants (cs ) changed from 0 to 4.0 mM. Fig. 1 (A) gives the rich phase changes of all the samples. It was found that: the groups of SDS and SDC showed the similar phase sequences. In the case of SDS group, the solution was kept transparent when the cs < 0.9 mM. The turbid phase appeared when the cs reached to 0.9 (a7), 1.0 (a8) and 2.0 (a9) mM, meaning the formation of a large number of vesicles. When the cs was higher than 2.0 mM, the turbid phase disappeared. SDC group showed the turbid phase only when cs was at 0.9 (b7) mM. While there was no obvious turbid phase appearing in all the samples of AOT. Subsequently, the extraction efficiency (E%) experiments were conducted. Pure CQDs showed the poor ability of Au(III) extraction that, shown in Fig. 1(B), the value was lower than 30%. Compared with that, the extraction efficiency (E%) was greatly improved with addition of surfactants. The group with SDS shows the great extraction ability to extract Au(III) when the cs was at 2.0 mM, reaching 96.8% of extraction efficiency (E%). This result informed that 0.500 g L−1 CQDs with 2.0 mM SDS could extract Au(III) almost. Then, the sample (0.500 g L−1 CQDs with 2.0 mM SDS) was selected for the next-step research to investigate the extraction behavior under different influence factors.

To evaluate the behaviors of the SDS/CQDs system in extraction, the experimental conditions including equilibrium time, concentration of vesicles, salinity and pH on extraction efficiency (E%) were applied.

The data shown in Fig. 2 (A) demonstrates that the equilibrium time was within 5 min, indicating 5 min is sufficient enough for the extraction equilibrium. The concentrations of CQDs were applied to investigate the extraction efficiency (E%) were from 0.063 to 0.563 g L−1 (SDS from 0.252 to 2.252 mM). As shown in Fig. 2(B), the curve attained a plateau fluctuating at around 96% when the concentration of CQDs was higher than 0.375 g L−1. Herein, 0.375 g L−1 was considered as the balance dot revealing this concentration of vesicles could load almost all the Au(III).

Behavior in acidities on extraction efficiency (E%) can be divided into 3 parts. Seen from the Fig. 2(C), the extraction efficiency (E%) was higher than 90% when the c(H+) was between 1 × 10−9 ∼ 1 × 10−5 mM (pH = 5 ∼ 9). The maximum of 96.48% showed at c(H+) = 1.0 × 10−3. When the c(H+) was higher than 1 × 10−3 mM (pH > 3), the existence of H+ was considered as the single factor that could cause the decline of E% by causing the hydrolysis of SDS into corresponding acid [35]. This could be explained by the dark brown precipitation showing in the bottom. And then, when the c(H+) is lower than 1.0 × 10−9 mM, more OH− was added to neutralize the H+. With more OH− existing in the system after neutralization, AuCl4 − was transformed into [AuCl4-n(OH)n]− gradually, a kind of rapidly formed hydroxyl-containing ions substituting the Cl− of the gold complexes stepwise [36]. Otherwise, residual OH− was considered as the one competitor of Au(III) [35]. At last, to make it clear that halide ions on extraction efficiency (E%), NaBr and NaCl were used. Overall, negative correlations were shown between extraction efficiency (E%) and the amount of NaCl/NaBr, shown in Fig. 2(D). Because of the same valence state with AuCl4 − and smaller molecular volume, a competition between AuCl4 − and halide ions was initially assumed [37].

In summary, during extraction process, the vesicles combined with Au(III) were more “active” in the mild conditions with low acidity and salinity. The behavior shown above aroused our great interests to investigate the mechanism behind phenomenon.

For further determination of the mechanism, zeta potential and size distribution analysis were applied to characterize the SDS group. The continuous change of zeta potential and size distribution are shown in Fig. 3 (A). The zeta potential of the vesicles with the best extraction efficiency (E%) was −14.8 mV, meaning the vesicles are negatively charged. And the average diameter of vesicles was around 350 nm (Fig. 3(B)), proving that the conjugates could be almost filtered by the filter membrane with pore size of 0.22 μm.

Fig. 3(E)–(G) shows the morphology of vesicles at the concentration of 2.0 mM SDS and 0.500 g L−1 CQDs. Even the double-bilayer-membrane could be observed by TEM. However, there were no big differences of size distribution and zeta potential before and after extraction, as shown in Fig. 3(C) and (D). So, it is assumed that anion exchange process takes place during the extraction of Au(III). Part of Br− which were originally surrounding the CQDs is replaced by the AuCl4 −.

Before the determination of the coordination number, the image of the CQDs was captured by HR-TEM, shown in Fig. 4 (A). There was a large quantity of quasi-spherical objects with an average size of ∼2.8 nm, which could be assigned to CQDs [27,38]. According to calculation based on previous research reported, the theoretical ratio of AuCl4 −:CQDs is determined to be approximately 6.

Job’s method, which is also called continuous variation method, was applied to determine the coordination number accurately. Herein, the total amount of the AuCl4 − and the CQDs is fixed at 0.028 mM with the molar ratio of AuCl4 −:CQDs changing continuously. The ratio of Au(III):CQDs ranges from 1 to 19. Results were shown in Fig. 4(B). By plotting the molar ratio of Au(III): CQDs as a function of the equilibrium concentration of Au(III) loading at vesicles (c Au(III),v ). When the molar ratio of Au: CQDs was around 6, the c Au(III),v reaches to the maximum value of 13.80 mM, which indicates that the ratio of Au(III): CQD is about 6:1.

1H NMR analysis was performed to confirm if there was any change of the structure of the vesicles before and after extraction. The 1H NMR spectra of vesicle with and without Au(III) shown in Fig. 4(C), which has been processed in DMSO-d 6. The spectra of CQDs are obtained from previous research [26], indicating no big change in the number and shape of the peaks. Fig. 4(C) showed the interaction between imidazole and AuCl4 −. The chemical shifts of the hydrogen atoms nearest to the nitrogen atoms are more affected after extraction: B-B’ 7.874 → 7.817/7.755 → 7.752, C-C’ 4.332 → 4.280. Compared with that, chemical shifts farther from the nitrogen atoms are less affected: A-A’ 9.361 → 9.362, D-D’ 3.876 → 3.872, E-E’ 3.684 → 3.683. This information proves that the mechanism of extraction is based on the anion-exchange process, which could be expressed vividly by Scheme 2 .

The mechanism of the extraction helps clarifying the phenomenon with different factors: (1) when NaCl/NaBr are added into the solution, the extraction process was disturbed because of the competition between Au(III) and halide ions. Due to the same valence state and smaller molecular volume, halide ions replaced the position of AuCl4 −. With more addition of NaBr/NaCl, this influence is more enhanced. (2) So, there were two effects caused by HCl. The first one is that H+ would cause the hydrolysis of SDS to break the structure of vesicles; the second is that the Cl− is competing with AuCl4 −, which has the similar competitive effects with OH−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of gold by reductive stripping
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous paper, a convenient way to strip Au(III) from the extraction complex was applied [35]. Firstly, filter membranes loaded with vesicles@Au(III) were mixed with NH3·H2O under ultrasonic for 2 h to convert the AuCl4 − into the positively charged Au(NH3)4 3+. The solution was analyzed by flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (FAAS) to make sure the existence of Au(III). Secondly, 0.1 g NaBH4 was added into the mixture, then the solution turned purple with purple precipitation. Finally, the concentration of Au(III) in the mixture was detected by FAAS at ∼1 mg/L, proving there was less Au(III) existing. This result proves that the Au(III) loading on the vesicles is stripped successfully from the complex.

Fluorescent properties of the CQD-based vesicles are the most outstanding character different from other compounded systems formed by surfactants. And the fluorescence could not be quenched by the metal ions like: Ag+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+ [26]. These ions appeared frequently in the second resources with Au(III) [39]. Because there is a fast interaction between vesicles and Au(III), and extraction rate decreased slightly under low acidity (pH ≈ 3), these are basic of the rapid detection and reliability of test results. According to the previous work [40], the excitation wavelength of 370 nm was selected for the fluorescent intensity of the vesicles. And the appearances of the vesicles were observed by the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp [40].

To explore the fluorescence change of the vesicles with addition of Au(III). Equivalent volumes (2 mL) of vesicles (0.400 g L−1) and AuCl4 − (0.243 mM) were mixed. Fluorescent intensity of each group was detected after vibration. The equilibrium time is short in normal vibration speed, which makes the continuous change of fluorescent intensity difficult to observe. So, the equilibrium time was prolonged to ∼40 min by adjusting the vibration speed lower.

Fig. 5 (A) shows the continuous decreasing fluorescence intensity of the vesicles with combination of AuCl4 −. It informs that: each curve reaches to the maximum value when the emission wavelength is equal to 460 nm. With the vibration time increasing, the more AuCl4 − combined with vesicles, the less the fluorescent intensity could be detected. Fig. 5(B) showed that variation of the maximum PL intensity excited at 460 nm as a function of vibration time (T). Compared with that, there was no big change of fluorescence of the pure vesicle sample in all the 9 samples, which proves that the structure of vesicles is stable enough to maintain the fluorescent intensity. And the residual Au(III) in water was detected, shown in Fig. 5(C). We can conclude that: with the vibration time increasing, the more AuCl4 − combined with vesicles, the less the fluorescent intensity could be detected. And this continuous change could be viewed by our naked eyes under 365 nm UV lamp, shown in Fig. 5(D). It should be noted that, this process could be finished under normal vibration speed within 5 min.

Based on that, instead of simulating the linear relationship between Au(III) and PL intensity, we designed a method to detect Au(III) by using the fluorescence off–on strategy. That is: when the vesicle was mixed with lower concentration of Au(III), the fluorescence is still “on”; when the vesicle was mixed with a higher one, the fluorescent intensity is “off”. If the concentration of Au(III) which could exactly “quench” the fluorescence completely was known, the concentration range of Au(III) solution causing the fluorescence “off” would be detected.

Considering the result that the Au(III) solution of 0.243 mM could be extracted by 0.400 g L−1 vesicles completely, the basic concentration ratio is set based on the 0.243 mM AuCl4 − and 0.400 g L−1 CQDs to make sure the stability and credibility of the results.

The basic concentration of each groups of vesicles/Au(III) varies from 0.040/0.024, 0.080/0.049, 0.120/0.073, 0.160/0.097, 0.200/0.122, 0.240/0.146, 0.280/0.170, 0.320/0.194, 0.360/0.219, to 0.400 g L−1/0.243 mM. And then, a series of Au(III) solution (2 mL) whose concentration was surrounding the basic Au(III) solution were mixed with the same equivalent volume of basic vesicle solution. After vibration for 5 min, the fluorescence of each sample was detected to select the one could exactly quench the fluorescence completely.

After couples of experiments, value of 0 was hardly achieved. However, the intensity could decline to a value below 100 when the concentration of Au(III) was higher enough, and then the fluorescence couldn't be seen by naked eyes under the irradiation of UV lamp (365 nm). According to that, the intensity of the system below 100 was defined as the “quenching” concentration. At the same time, the concentration of Au(III) which exactly make the system fluorescent intensity below 100 was defined as the corresponding “quenching” concentration. As it is shown in Fig. 6 (A) and (B) that 10 different “quenching” concentration of Au(III) were found and listed below. Accordingly, the unknown concentration of Au(III) would be detected rapidly by mixing the Au(III) and a certain concentration of vesicles. After vibration for 5 min, the change of appearance of the system reflect the amount of Au(III). Viewed by naked eyes under the irradiation of the UV lamp (365 nm), or analysis through the PL spectrophotometer, if the fluorescent intensity is higher than 100 or the fluorescence is still “on”, it proves that the concentration of Au(III) is lower than the corresponding “quenching” Au(III) solution. If the fluorescent intensity is lower than 100 or the fluorescence is “off”, it proves that the concentration is higher than the corresponding “quenching” Au(III) solution. Fig. 7 shows the picture of appearance with/without Au(III) in room light and under UV lamp. A great advantage worthy to be pointed out is that the concentration gradient of the basic vesicle solution and the interval could be set according to the actual requirement.

A novel kind of fluorescent vesicle based on the self-aggregation of CQDs and SDS was constructed for gold(III) extraction. Under the combination of 0.500 g L−1 CQDs and 2 mM SDS, 96.8 % Au(III) could be extracted by vesicles. Through the TEM, spherical structures of around 350 nm was observed. The anion-exchange mechanism was proposed by studying the behavior and zeta potential analysis. By using Job s method, the coordination number of CQDs: Au(III) was confirmed as 1: 6. At last, we designed a flexible visual way to detect the concentration of Au(III) in aqueous solution by the application of the photoluminescence off–on mechanism. With given concentration of vesicles (0.040, 0.080, 0.120···0.360, 0.400 g L−1), the corresponding concentration of Au(III) solution to quench the fluorescence was obtained (0.024, 0.044, 0.063, 0.097, 0.126, 0.151, 0.175, 0.199, 0.219 and 0.233 mM). The concentration gradient of the basic vesicle solution and the interval could be set according to the actual requirements. The whole fluorescent change process could be observed by naked eyes. Approximate amount of Au(III) in aqueous system would be easily determined by watching the fluorescence of mixture quench.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21476129).
